summary: DHH argues that while the 'we're like family' rhetoric at work is rightly criticized, people can't then expect that underperforming colleagues should be protected from being cut. He endorses Netflix's 'professional team' metaphor, where people earn their spot through competence and fit. Using soccer as an extended analogy, he argues that finding the right level matters more than clinging to a team where you don't belong. He frames letting someone go as potentially merciful rather than cruel, and positions decisive personnel decisions as a manager's most important job.
key_points: The 'we're a family' workplace rhetoric is cynically used to extract sacrifice without mutual obligation
Netflix's 'professional team' metaphor is more honest — you earn your spot and can lose it
You can't reject family rhetoric while also expecting underperformers to be protected from cuts
Being on the wrong team hurts the individual too — cutting them can be an act of mercy, not cruelty
A manager's most important job is knowing when to make the cut and having the strength to do it
money_quotes: I've always hated the saying "we're like family here" when it comes to work. Because it's obviously not true, and it's usually cynically invoked by management to entice an undue obligation of sacrifice.
You can't have it both ways. You can't scorn the use of "we're a family" and then also expect that colleagues who can't keep up are spared the cut.
Now the natural delusion of every business owner is to think that they're running Manchester United when in fact they're barely hanging on in third division.
Cutting them from the team would be an act of mercy, not cruelty.
This is perhaps the most important job of the coach or manager. Knowing when to make the cut, and having the strength to do it.
Not everyone is meant to be Messi or to play on his team. But we wouldn't have much of a sport if his level was the only level.
Find your level, find a team that fits with it, and play to the best of your ability. That's the beautiful game.
key_insight: Rejecting the 'family' metaphor at work means accepting the 'team' metaphor's corollary: not everyone is guaranteed a spot, and decisive cuts are a manager's most important responsibility.
